result:
The first time I tasted a sun-warmed tomato straight from the vine, I understood time is edible. That tiny globe burst with summers my grandmother carried across an ocean in her apron pocket, seeds sewn like secret coins into the hem. She planted them in alien soil, spoke to them in a language the wind here had never heard, and they answered by reddening.  

I keep her tarnished pruning shears in my kitchen drawer, between the corkscrew and the flashlight no one trusts. Their blades still hold a ghost of her lavender lotion; when I open the drawer fast enough, the scent escapes like a paroled memory. Some evenings I take them out and snip nothing, just listen to the hinge whisper the way her knees once did kneeling between rows.  

Outside, my own vines reach through the fence toward the neighbor’s prettier life, stretching green fingers into their manicured silence. I let them trespass. Perhaps their fruit will surprise someone else with a past they never lived, a circular migration of sweetness.  

We call it heirloom, as if generations can be locked inside cellulose walls. But I think it’s the other way around: the tomato keeps us, carries our thin skins forward, repeats our flaws in ever-softening tissue. When I slice one open, the seeds gel like tiny glass eyes, watching me decide whether to swallow or plant. Either way, something of me will be rewritten in the dirt, watered by whatever skies are left, seasoned by the salt of ancestors I will someday join underground, feeding the next root that remembers.

message.content:
The first time I tasted a sun-warmed tomato straight from the vine, I understood time is edible. That tiny globe burst with summers my grandmother carried across an ocean in her apron pocket, seeds sewn like secret coins into the hem. She planted them in alien soil, spoke to them in a language the wind here had never heard, and they answered by reddening.  

I keep her tarnished pruning shears in my kitchen drawer, between the corkscrew and the flashlight no one trusts. Their blades still hold a ghost of her lavender lotion; when I open the drawer fast enough, the scent escapes like a paroled memory. Some evenings I take them out and snip nothing, just listen to the hinge whisper the way her knees once did kneeling between rows.  

Outside, my own vines reach through the fence toward the neighbor’s prettier life, stretching green fingers into their manicured silence. I let them trespass. Perhaps their fruit will surprise someone else with a past they never lived, a circular migration of sweetness.  

We call it heirloom, as if generations can be locked inside cellulose walls. But I think it’s the other way around: the tomato keeps us, carries our thin skins forward, repeats our flaws in ever-softening tissue. When I slice one open, the seeds gel like tiny glass eyes, watching me decide whether to swallow or plant. Either way, something of me will be rewritten in the dirt, watered by whatever skies are left, seasoned by the salt of ancestors I will someday join underground, feeding the next root that remembers.
